entry.title: Adding a Custom Column in Fiori Elements (List Report + Object Page)
entry.content:
Introduction 

The purpose of this blog post is to specify the elements to add in order to set up this custom column, and more specifically to understand the path to configure in the manifest when you want to modify a table, because using this extension in an association facet of an Object Page can be more complicated to understand.
To do so, two elements must be implemented: first, the column definition through an XML fragment, and second, its registration in the manifest file.

I had to use this extension because I was asked to customize the data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing the color depending on the displayed value.
This blog post also aims to specify when to use this extension and why it is preferable not to use it, along with an example of a regression I experienced during its addition.

This column can be added either to a List Report page or to an Object Page. Each table type has its own extension name, and the implementation differs slightly depending on the table type.

       Why use this extension as a last resort?

In this blog post, we will look at how to add a custom column. However, other approaches can often meet the requirement. This extension should be considered only when no standard alternative is available, for the following reasons:

  • Fiori Elements Flexibility: Some requirements can be fulfilled through manifest customization or OData annotations without writing any JavaScript or XML code.
  • Maintainability: View extensions are custom code that must be maintained whenever the Fiori Elements framework is updated, whereas standard annotations are automatically handled by SAP.
  • Regression Risk: A poorly implemented extension can interfere with native table features such as sorting, filtering, Excel export, and P13n personalization.

Example of a regression I encountered with this extension:

While developing my XML, I needed to add a specific width to my column. However, by defining this width in pixels (px), I caused a regression in the native behavior, which uses rem units. For example, the display variants were altered depending on the screen size, because rem units rely on a relative value, unlike pixels which impose a fixed size.

      Table Types and Associated Extensions

Fiori Elements supports several table types. Each has its own extension name that must be declared in the manifest.

Table Type 

Extension Name 

Description

ResponsiveTable

ResponsiveTableColumnsExtension

ResponsiveTableCellsExtension

Responsive table adapted for mobile and desktop (default)

GridTable

GridTableColumnsExtension

Table with fixed columns, suitable for large datasets

TreeTable

TreeTableColumnsExtension

Hierarchical (tree-like) table

Important: For a ResponsiveTable, two extensions are required: one for the column header and one for the cell content.

How to Implement the Column?

     XML Extension

To add this column, you need to define a fragment for the view extension. Create a file in the webapp/ext/fragments/ folder to add any desired column.

In this fragment, you define the desired properties of the column (position, displayed element, etc.). You can also add P13N parameters, such as sorting, to enable users to sort the column.

Example XML extension:

 

      Manifest Extension

In the manifest.json file, declare the view extension and the application parameters. Create an extension corresponding to the table type on the desired entity, then specify the fragment name and type.

Example Manifest:

Result :

After adding the XML extension and declaring it in the manifest.json, a new column appears in the table. In the example, the column customizes data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing colors based on displayed values.

The document also highlights a date-coloring scenario where different colors indicate whether a deadline is distant, approaching, or overdue. In such cases, a view extension is generally unnecessary because OData Criticality annotations can provide the same functionality in a standard, Fiori Elements–compliant way. 

Therefore, this standard approach should be preferred whenever they satisfy the requirement.

Adding a Column to an Object Page

      Differences Compared to a List Report

An Object Page can contain multiple sections, each with its own table. The main differences are:

Aspect

List Report

Object Page

Target View 

ListReport.view.ListReport

ObjectPage.view.Details

Extension key

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet + FacetID

Number of Tables 

One main table

Multiple tables (one per section)

Configuration Scope 

Entity-wide

Specific to each section/facet

      Example in an Object Page

In this example, a custom column is added to a Responsive Table within the Object Page of the Category entity. 

The custom column contains an Order button that is displayed only when the product stock is below 30 units. This behavior relies on business logic that cannot be implemented using standard Fiori Elements annotations. 

As with the Grid Table example, an extension fragment is created for the column definition:

However, because this is a Responsive Table, a second extension is required to define the column header:

Since the extension is used within an Object Page, the manifest.json configuration differs slightly from a List Report. You must target the ObjectPage.view.Details view and specify the corresponding facet in the extension key to indicate which table should receive the custom column.

The point I spent time on due to a lack of understanding: 'TypeTable' corresponds to the CDS of our table and not the CDS of the Object Page. In our example, 'Products' is a CDS association (added in a 'ProductsFacet' facet) of our application, which is based on the 'Category' CDS.


Result: 

 

Purpose of the Extension

     When Should You Use This Extension?

This extension is relevant in the following scenarios:

  • Complex conditional display: color, icon, or text based on multi-field business logic that cannot be expressed through annotations.
  • Non-standard UI5 component: action button, link, image, slider, or any component not natively supported by UI.DataField.
  • External data integration: displaying data coming from another JSON model or a separate REST API call. 
  • Frontend-side calculation: combining or calculating values using a JavaScript formatter. 

     Best Practices 

  • Always prefix IDs: avoid conflicts with IDs generated by Fiori Elements. 
  • Use i18n: never hardcode header texts; always use entries from the i18n.properties file.
  • Keep JavaScript out of fragments: complex logic should be implemented in a dedicated formatter.js file, not inline in the XML.
  • Test P13n (personalization): ensure that the custom column behaves correctly with sorting, filtering, and show/hide functionalities.

entry.title: How to Update a Custom Field In SAP CRM Using Function Module CRM ORDER MAINTAIN
entry.content:
Introduction:


When working in SAP CRM  , a common requirement is to  programmatically update custom fields (extension fields or KUT fields) on Service orders or other Business transactions . These fields are typically added via Custom Fields and Logic  in Fiori Applications and are stored in Standard Tables . (For example :  Service Order Table CRMS4D_SERV_H)

The challenge many developers face is: how do you correctly update these fields without triggering inconsistencies, locking issues, or lost data?

In this blog post, I'll walk you through a working ABAP function module that correctly updates a custom  number field (ZZ1_CUSTOMFIELD_SRH)  on a CRM service order header — using the standard CRM order maintenance APIs.

 Lets Get Started :
The Approach: Using Standard CRM APIs
SAP CRM has its own internal framework for reading and writing order data. You should never update CRM tables like CRMS4D_SERV_H  directly via UPDATE   statements  , Doing so bypasses the CRM Business Object Layer  and Cause Data Inconsistencies

The correct sequence is:

  1. Get an internal handle using  (CRM_INTLAY_GET_HANDLE )
  2. Prepare the customer header data structure ( CRMT_CUSTOMER_H_COM)
  3. Specify which fields you are changing via the input fields table (CRMT_INPUT_FIELD_TAB )
  4. Call ( CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN )to apply changes in the CRM buffer
  5. Call ( CRM_ORDER_SAVE ) to persist the changes
  6. Commit with ( BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT ) or rollback on failure

Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough
Step 1 – Read the Existing Order Header

SELECT SINGLE * FROM crms4d_serv_h
    INTO @DATA(ls_res)
    WHERE header_guid = @iv_guid.

We first read the service order header to confirm it exists and retrieve the  header_guid for later use in the input fields structure.
Step 2 – Get a CRM Internal Handle

CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_INTLAY_GET_HANDLE'
    IMPORTING
      ev_handle = ev_ref_handle.

The handle is a unique reference used internally by the CRM framework to track your changes within the current session buffer. It must be consistent across all structures you fill.

Step 3 – Fill the Customer Header Structure

  ls_customer_h-ref_handle         = ev_ref_handle.
  ls_customer_h-ref_guid           = iv_guid.
  ls_customer_h-mode               = 'A'.
  ls_customer_h-zz1_customfield_srh = iv_custom_field.
APPEND ls_customer_h TO lt_customerh.

The  CRMT_CUSTOMER_H_COM  structure holds all custom extension fields for the service order header. Set  mode = 'A'  to update the field.

Step 4 – Define the Input Fields (What Changed)

ls_nametab = 'ZZ1_CUSTOMFIELD_SRH'.
APPEND ls_nametab TO lt_nametab.

ls_input_fields-ref_handle  = ev_ref_handle.
ls_input_fields-ref_guid    = ls_res-header_guid.
ls_input_fields-ref_kind    = 'A'.
ls_input_fields-objectname  = 'CUSTOMER_H'.
ls_input_fields-field_names = lt_nametab[].
INSERT ls_input_fields INTO TABLE et_input_fields.

This is a critical step that many developers miss. CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN uses the ct_input_fields table to know which specific fields have been changed. If you don't include a field name here, the system will ignore that field — even if you've filled it in the data structure.
ref_kind  = 'A' refers to the order header
objectname = 'CUSTOMER_H' maps to the customer header extension object

Step 5 – Call CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN

CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN'
  EXPORTING
    it_customer_h   = lt_customerh
  CHANGING
    ct_input_fields = et_input_fields
  EXCEPTIONS
    error_occurred    = 1
    document_locked   = 2
    no_change_allowed = 3
    no_authority      = 4
    OTHERS            = 5.

This function writes your changes into the CRM in-memory buffer. It does not save to the database yet. Always handle the exceptions - especially (document_locked ), which means another user or process has the order open.
Step 6 – Save and Commit (or Rollback)

CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_ORDER_SAVE'
  EXPORTING
    it_objects_to_save = lt_guid
  IMPORTING
    et_saved_objects   = lt_saved_objects
  EXCEPTIONS
    document_not_saved = 1
    OTHERS             = 2.

IF sy-subrc = 0.
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
    EXPORTING
      wait = 'X'.
ELSE.
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_ROLLBACK'.
ENDIF.

Always use wait = 'X' in  ( BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT ) to ensure the commit is synchronous before the calling program continues. If the save fails, roll back immediately to avoid partial data corruption.
COMPLETE FUNCTION MODULE FOR REFERENCE : 

FUNCTION CUSTOMFIELD.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*"*"Local Interface:
*"  IMPORTING
*"     REFERENCE(IV_GUID) TYPE CRMT_OBJECT_GUID
*"     REFERENCE(IV_CONTRACT_ACC) TYPE BAPICAPARA-ACCOUNT
*"     REFERENCE(IV_PROCESS_TYPE) TYPE CRMT_PROCESS_TYPE_DB OPTIONAL
*"     REFERENCE(IV_CUSTOMFIELD) TYPE ZZ1_CUSTOMFIELD
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------

  SELECT SINGLE * FROM crms4d_serv_h
    INTO @DATA(ls_res)
    WHERE header_guid = @iv_guid.

  DATA ls_input_fields TYPE crmt_input_field.
  DATA lt_nametab TYPE crmt_input_field_names_tab.
  DATA ls_nametab TYPE crmt_input_field_names.
  DATA ls_orderadm_h TYPE crmt_orderadm_h_com.
  DATA ev_ref_handle TYPE crmt_handle.

  DATA: lt_customerh    TYPE crmt_customer_h_comt,
        ls_customer_h   TYPE crmt_customer_h_com,
        et_orderadm_h   TYPE crmt_orderadm_h_comt,
        et_input_fields TYPE crmt_input_field_tab,
        lt_orderadmh    TYPE crmt_orderadm_h_comt,
        lt_guid         TYPE crmt_object_guid_tab,
        lt_saved_objects TYPE crmt_return_objects,
        ls_guid         TYPE crmt_object_guid,
        es_return       TYPE bapiret2,
        lt_orderadm_i   TYPE crmt_orderadm_i_comt,
        ls_orderadm_i   TYPE crmt_orderadm_i_com.

  " Get handle
  CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_INTLAY_GET_HANDLE'
    IMPORTING
      ev_handle = ev_ref_handle.

  CLEAR ls_orderadm_h.
  CLEAR: ls_customer_h,
         ls_nametab,
         ls_input_fields,
         lt_customerh[],
         lt_nametab[],
         et_input_fields[].

  ls_customer_h-ref_handle         = ev_ref_handle.
  ls_customer_h-ref_guid           = iv_guid.
  ls_customer_h-mode               = 'A'.
  ls_customer_h-zz1_customfield_srh = iv_customfield.

  
  ls_nametab = 'ZZ1_CUSTOMFIELD_SRH'.

  APPEND ls_customer_h TO lt_customerh.
  APPEND ls_nametab TO lt_nametab.

  CLEAR ls_input_fields.
  ls_input_fields-ref_handle  = ev_ref_handle.
  ls_input_fields-ref_guid    = ls_res-header_guid.
  ls_input_fields-ref_kind    = 'A'.
  ls_input_fields-objectname  = 'CUSTOMER_H'.
  ls_input_fields-field_names = lt_nametab[].

  INSERT ls_input_fields INTO TABLE et_input_fields.

  CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN'
    EXPORTING
      it_customer_h = lt_customerh
    CHANGING
      ct_input_fields = et_input_fields
    EXCEPTIONS
      error_occurred   = 1
      document_locked = 2
      no_change_allowed = 3
      no_authority     = 4
      OTHERS          = 5.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    WRITE: sy-subrc.
  ELSE.
    CLEAR lt_guid[].
    ls_guid = iv_guid.
    APPEND ls_guid TO lt_guid.

    CALL FUNCTION 'CRM_ORDER_SAVE'
      EXPORTING
        it_objects_to_save = lt_guid
      IMPORTING
        et_saved_objects   = lt_saved_objects
      EXCEPTIONS
        document_not_saved = 1
        OTHERS             = 2.
    
     IF sy-subrc = 0.
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
      EXPORTING
        wait = 'X'.
  ELSE.
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_ROLLBACK'.
  ENDIF.

    
  ENDIF.

ENDFUNCTION.

Conclusion: 

By following this guide, you have successfully learned how to programmatically update a custom extension field in SAP CRM Web UI using the standard ABAP order maintenance framework. You created a function module that correctly uses CRM_INTLAY_GET_HANDLE, CRM_ORDER_MAINTAIN, and CRM_ORDER_SAVE — ensuring data consistency without bypassing the CRM business object layer.

If you have any questions or faced challenges, feel free to drop a comment below.

entry.title: Your Journey to the Autonomous Enterprise: Unlocking Value with SAP GROW and RISE with SAP
entry.content:
Cloud is more than a technology shift—it’s a business transformation opportunity. Join this webcast to explore how RISE with SAP and SAP GROW empower organizations to modernize ERP, increase agility, and advance toward an autonomous enterprise with greater speed and confidence.

Speaker: David Yawalkar, Head of RISE with SAP - Package & Commercialization Adoption, SAP

Link to Session: https://events.sap.com/sap-user-groups/en_us/executive_insights.html#261013

Add to Calendar: https://assets.dm.ux.sap.com/sap-user-groups/261013_your_journey_to_the_autonomous_sap_grow_rise_with_sap.ics

entry.title: SAP Developer News, July 9th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

Live from WeAreDevelopers

- WeAreDevelopers World Congress https://www.wearedevelopers.com/world-congress

CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)

- Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/jun26

ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!

- https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPOSS.abap-cleaner

Live streams at Code Connect next week

- Code Connect https://code-connect.dev/
- UI5con https://openui5.org/ui5con/
- UI5con agenda https://openui5.org/ui5con/program.html
- UI5con 2026 Live from the Main Stage https://www.youtube.com/live/CMPudw4scSE
- reCAP https://recap-conf.dev/
- reCAP agenda https://recap-conf.dev/program.html
- reCAP Live from the Main Stage https://broadcast.sap.com/go/reCAP
- reCAP 2026 - Streaming Sidetrack https://teams.microsoft.com/meet/347149883889697?p=ZkR4erzGjpPRtZ6zJh

Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway

- Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/building-mcp-servers-with-sap-integration-suite-what-to-design-not-just-how/ba-p/14435261
- Design APIs and MCP servers https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/design-apis?locale=en-US
- Release calendar for SAP Integration Suite https://me.sap.com/notes/3418154

Did You Know? #25 cds add test

- The cds add command https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/tools/cds-cli#cds-add

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro
00:07 Live from WeAreDevelopers
02:16 CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)
13:14 ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!
14:37 Live streams at Code Connect next week
15:05 Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway
17:20 DYK #25 cds add test

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the Developer News for July 9th 2026.

[Tom, Sheena, Shilpa, Witalij]
Hi, we're coming to you this week from WeAreDevelopers conference in Berlin, which is a little bit of a difference for us developer advocates because we're actually mostly attending a conference instead of delivering one. So it's our opportunity to connect with other developers, particularly non-SAP developers from all around the world. I'm giving a session on Friday with Michael Ameling, where I'll be doing a preview of the new developers.sap.com that's all AI-powered - we'll show you more of in the coming weeks. What are the rest of you looking forward to?

Yeah we just now attended one session on Microsoft Fabric, it was really exciting, and we also got to know the latest advanced technologies that we can look forward to. So it was really exciting to be part of that session. The line was a little too long to get into the session.

That is the challenging part, to get into the sessions, but for me the interesting part is AI and agents because, it is not a surprise, but to be outside of the SAP ecosystem is what I'm interested in too, and get to learn more of what's happening outside of SAP.

So have you tried to find any sessions without AI or agents in the name?

No, all agents. I think that was the qualifier, probably. OK, and for me it's actually not the first time I'm attending this conference. I was here in 2019 and it is really good to be back here. This time SAP has a big presence here, we are actually one of the sponsors, we have our place on the show floor, we have our sessions as Thomas mentioned, he's going to do one with Michael Ameling but as well we have some other SAP colleagues delivering sessions based on the experience from their work, working with UI for example, and with user experience in this exciting AI era. So all in all, "no coffee, no code" but unfortunately the line for coffee is the longest one here, so if you don't see me coding this week, that's just because I cannot stand in this line and get my coffee.

[DJ]
cds 10 is out! That is, the next major release of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, also known as cds 10, was published last week.

This particular major release marks many milestones and is packed full of goodness.

Within the limitations of these news items, I can only give a brief overview, and you should definitely head over to the release notes which are very readable and full of details.

Some of the features and highlights are marked as Alpha or Beta ... so please pay attention to these status badges when working through the content.

Now, like other releases, the notes for this release are organised into different categories. There's the main major release upgrade category of course, a set of important highlights, notes on changes and improvements for both the Node.js and Java runtimes, and some updates for multitenancy services as well.

So, let's start with the very essence of this major release - the upgrade and migration context, and what tht implies.

Each of the packages that make up the entire framework have had major release version upgrades.
As the release notes say, don't mix old and new major versions. Instead, embrace change and try out the new cds upgrade tool which can help you.

The runtime support is also changing, with both Node.js and Java.

And before leaving this upgrade context, I must highlight the facilities that the CAP team have put together for this essential part of the software lifecycle - upgrades and migration. There's a new Migration Guide to help you get to cds 10 ... and a bleeding edge new 'cds upgrade' facility, in Alpha, to help with upgrades.

So next, let's have a look at some of the highlights, of which there are many in this release. Some of them are either in Alpha or Beta status, but they're there!

Let me enumerate them briefly. There's now support for aggregate data in hierarchies, useful for product catalogs, org charts, bills of materials and similar scenarios. It's available right now with the Java runtime, and support for Node.js is coming soon.

For VS Code users there's a new Project Explorer to help you visually navigate through your project and model's structures.

Now, while this has been hinted at before this advent of cds 10, there's a new protocol which I'm going to describe as "CAP native", and that's now available, and it's called HCQL - CQL conveyed over HTTP. Well suited for CAP-to-CAP integrations, and available just by adding an @hcql annotation, in the true CAP tradition of magic-made-simple.

There are early AI related goodies too, in the form of CAP-level (Calesi-style) agents, and also a CAP skills library, because, as we know, focusing on intent and best practice in development is essential.

There's a new Data Inspector, which comes in the form of a plugin and allows the controlled inspection of data in the entities exposed by your services.

Now, event queues in CAP have also been given lots of love, in the form of a new scheduling API, improved processing, and an overhaul of the documentation.

Now, last December we saw the first public Beta of the 'cds export' command which we can use to create API client packages from CDS service definitions. With this cds 10 release, that command is now generally available.

Now there are some more highlights in this cds 10 release, but my good friend and colleague Antonio is going to cover those in another news item in this news episode, so stay tuned!

Let me now turn to the Node.js runtime context where there are also plenty of items for us to celebrate.

First up, consolidated service APIs. What does that mean? Well, the generic CRUD (Create Read Update Delete) handler now returns the same shape of data for all write operations. That means app, db and HCQL-proxied remote services all look and behave the same way, which is great for uniform and minimised handler code variations.

Now, AI agents are great, but one thing they don't know much about is Fiori draft data. So working with active data has been simplified and consolidated - making it easier for everyone, us humans and our AI overlords, to work in a draft-first context.

A massive piece of work that the team has undertaken over the last months, culminating in what we have with cds 10, is a significant move towards a greater embrace of modern tools and native libraries. Very briefly - the testing framework Vitest is now used in favour of Jest, which will be abandoned longer term. ECMAScript Modules (that's ESM to you and me) is the official standardised system for packages and reuse, and CAP is now embracing that as the default module system. Third party dependencies are being dropped in favour of native or built-in alternatives. For example this calendar year already has seen a move to a Node-native SQLite driver (which replaces better-sqlite3) and to the Node-native Fetch API (which replaces Axios, and in development scenarios, also removes the need for the SAP Cloud SDK).

This work to embrace modern techniques, native libraries and the newest stable standards is fantastic. An important "side effect" is also something we can celebrate - a significantly reduced set of dependencies (and thus a smaller install footprint and code surface area).

Now last but certainly not least in the Node.js category is some news on the Telemetry plugin, which is now at version 2 after a major update which also brings support for OpenTelemetry SDK 2.0.

Next, let's look at the Java runtime category, which has now reached major version 5. Lots of new features, including, importantly, support for Spring Boot 4.

There are some new minimum versions of major dependencies that you need to take care of.

With CAP Java 5 there are some changes that are not backwards compatible (as in any major release), but the migration is made easier for us with a specific migration guide and OpenRewrite recipes for automated code adjustments.

Now, there are changes to CDS property defaults to align them with recommended settings for production use.

The API methods that were deprecated in the previous major release and marked as 'forRemoval' ... have now been removed.

CAP Java 5 now defaults to JDK 25, which is a long-term-support version. The SapMachine 25 JDK with the SAP Java buildpack is recommended.

With this new major CAP Java release, search is harmonised with both CAP Node.js and RAP, and by default uses the SAP HANA search syntax (for which there is now full support). Fuzzy search is also now enabled by default on SAP HANA, with improved support for UUIDs and large strings, and available in hierarchy queries.

Now, there's also much faster typed data access available. Instead of using a dynamic proxy to handle the underlying data map for accessor interfaces, it's now possible to have implementation classes generated. This resulting in up to 10 times the performance.

Previously, CAP Java used Apache's Olingo library for OData support. Following Olingo's retirement last year, there's now built-in support for OData, that's no mean feat! That of course also means fewer dependencies and it also brings improved performance.

There's also support for creating active versions of draft-enabled entities, similar to what we learned about earlier in the Node.js runtime context.

There are also plenty more features that come with CAP Java 5. Check them out in the release notes.

But to round out the release notes overview for this cds 10 version, there are a couple of points of note in the context of MTX services. First, there's now a stricter annotation validation for extensions. And second, migration support to go from @SAP/cds-mtx to @SAP/cds-mtxs has been removed.

That pretty much wraps up things for this June 2026 major release, congratulations to the entire CAP team for a huge set of new features and improvements. And don't forget to check out Antonio's item in this news episode for those other highlights.

[Rich]
Hey friends, Rich Heilman here. I hope everyone is staying cool, staying out of the heat. Good news for ABAP developers this week. ABAP Cleaner is now available as a VS Code extension, bringing its powerful code formatting capabilities out of Eclipse and into the modern editor of choice for many developers. So, what does it do? ABAP Cleaner applies over 100 rules to your code with a single keyboard shortcut. Automatically cleaning up formatting, modernising syntax, and even flagging unused variables. The result is clean and consistent code across your entire team without anyone having to argue about style. You can run it as a quick one-shot format, or drop into interactive mode to choose exactly which rules apply to your codebase. If your team already has a configuration setup in Eclipse, you can import those rule variants directly. No need to start from scratch. The setup does a Java runtime and a bit of environment variable configuration, but once it's running, you get the same functionality that ABAP developers have relied on in ADT in Eclipse - now in a much lighter, faster editor. If you're doing ABAP development in VS Code, this one's worth installing. Check it out today.

[Ajay]
Hello everyone. Code Connect is next week, from 13th to 16th July 2026. For all of you who cannot join us in person, there are live streams available for UI5con and reCAP, from the main stage. Please check out the agenda and streaming links available for UI5con and reCAP and join us online during the Code Connect event. Links to the live streams and agendas will be available in the description.

[Antonio]
¡Hola, SAP Developers. As you already know, the June 2026 CAP release is huge. DJ has already shared many of the highlights and I just want to mention a couple more. There is a new AI plugin, in Alpha, which simplifies integration with SAP AI Core. It will automatically resolve service bindings, manage resource groups, and AI model deployments. And it provides a standard AI Core service to interact with AI Core programatically. The new Data Privacy plugin, in Beta, for Node.js, which provides out of the box integration with the SAP Data Privacy Integration Service. Based on the personal data and ILM annotations in the data model the plugin will automatically expose two endpoints that can be consumed by the service. Now, related to the integration space, CAP Node.js now supports the Event Mesh capability in SAP Integration Suite, as an alternative to the standalone SAP Event Mesh, meaning that you can now easily use it as an event broken in your CAP applications. There is the new MCP Protocol Adapter, which allows exposing existing CAP services to AI agents and LLM powered as just another protocol. Just install the plugin and annotate services with @McP, et voila, your CAP service exposes MCP functionality. Extremely powerful and easy to implement. I already used it in a CAP service that I'm developing while I was getting familiar with the new feature of SAP Integration Suite. Yes, what we've all been waiting for - the new MCP Gateway is now available as part of Integration Suite. It is part of the new Integration Cell Runtime which is a cloud native runtime purposely built for designing, deploying and operating APIs and MCP servers. It is GA now and being rolled out in the Enhanced and Premium editions of SAP Integration Suite. Not just that, but it is also available in the BTP Trial environment. What are yoiu waiting for? Go check it out. Hasta luego!

[Ajay]
Did you know that cds add does generate the best practices of the test implementation of your CAP service? One can rapidly create a sample CAP project with cds init bookshop --add sample. Run cds add test in the terminal to add test suite files for all the defined CAP services in your existing project. It creates the latest boilerplate files and structures required for you to get started with your test implementation in a jiffy. For each services it generates a sample test for the GET request for an entity and a test for any action defined in the service. This also adds the test dependencies to your project. It uses the Node test runner for the tests. Install your dependencies and just run npm t to execute your tests. The generated test implementation does need to be tweaked for the valid test data. CAP also provides a command npx cds test to mute the verbose server output and lets you focus only on the failing tests. Use -u to unmute the server output when you do not need them. Enjoy your CAP tests!

entry.title: From SEGW to @OData.publish: Expose Your CDS View as OData in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction:

This Blog2 is continuation of blog 1, refer below link:
Link to blog1 
 
In Blog1, we created a CDS view using tables Sales Order Header (VBAK) and Sales Order Items (VBAP). Now, in this Blog, we will make this data available for outside world. 
In S/4HANA, CDS views are not just used for reporting - they can also be exposed as OData service, which allows them to be consumed by external systems, SAP Fiori applications, API and integrations.
 
As an ABAP developer I have always used transaction code SEGW to manually create OData services. But now with CDS, this process is much simpler and faster.

 

Lets see how to expose CDS view as OData : Step by Step:

Step 1: Add OData Annotation

Open your CDS view and add anootation @odata.publish: true
Example below
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZSALEORDREP'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Creating my 1st CDS view'
@OData.publish: true

define view ZCDS_ORDVIEW
    as select from vbak as Header
  association [1..*] to vbap as Item
    on Header.vbeln = Item.vbeln
{
  key Header.vbeln as SalesOrder,
      Header.erdat as CreationDate,
      Header.netwr as NetAmount,
      Header.waerk as Currency,

      case Header.auart
        when '00' then 'Standard Order'
        else 'Other Order Type'
      end as OrderTypeDescription,

      Item
}

Step 2: Activate the CDS View

Press ctrl + F3 or icon activate to activate the CDS view. Once activated, SAP will generate the OData service with name <cdsviewname>_CDS.

In our example OData service name generated is ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .

Step 3: Activate the Service in SAP Gateway

Even though the service is generated, it must be activated in gateway system. Login to your gateway system and follow below steps:

  1. Go to transaction /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  2. Click Add Service
  3. Enter system alias as: your system name
  4. Search service: ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .
  5. Once its found, select the service and click button 'Add selected Services'.
  6. Assign a package. In my case I used local package $TMP.

 

 
 

Step 4: Test the OData Service

To test the OData service, in gateway system run transaction code /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE then it will list down all the services name under technical service name. Select your service ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS and click button SAP Client Gateway.  This will open new screen SAP gateway client. Refer below screenshots:
 
 

$metadata shows full structure like Fields, types, associations

Example: Use the following URL to filter a specific sales order: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$filter=SalesOrder eq '00XXXXX178' Note: Ensure there are no spaces around the '=' in the $filter parameter.


 /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW/$count

You can also use different OData query options like Top 5 records: ...?$top=5, Sort data: ...?$orderby=CreationDate desc etc. These options help in optimizing data retrieval and are widely used in Fiori applications.

To test using URL, use below format. For testing I have used Postman :

https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

 
The OData service can also be tested outside of SAP using tools like: - Browser,  Postman or Curl Example: https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

When you add @OData.publish: true:

  • SAP automatically creates an OData service
  • Associations become navigation properties
  • CDS fields are exposed as OData properties
  • No manual service modeling is required

This is why CDS is preferred in modern development.

Conclusion

Exposing CDS view as an OData service shows how simple and powerful modern ABAP development has become. With just a single annotation, you can turn your data model into a consumable service ready for applications, APIs, and integrations. What once required manual effort in SEGW can now be achieved in minutes using CDS. This not only improves productivity but also ensures consistency between your data model and service layer.

What’s Next?

In the next step, we’ll enhance this CDS view with UI annotations and generate a Fiori application without writing frontend code.

Let’s Discuss

Have you started exposing CDS views as OData services?

Are you still using SEGW in your projects?

Share your thoughts and experiences.

entry.title: End-to-End Ad-Hoc Procurement in SAP S/4HANA: Handling Custom Requisitions Through the 3-Way Match
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

I wanted to share a complete walkthrough of an ad-hoc procurement process I executed in an SAP S/4HANA environment. Instead of running a standard automated cycle, I wanted to see how the system handles an unexpected, text-based emergency request from the initial requisition all the way down to final financial accounting.

Here is the step-by-step breakdown of the lifecycle for an Emergency Industrial Cooling Fan Unit scenario:

1. The Purchase Requisition (PR) Stage

  • The Setup: Because this was a sudden, non-standard emergency request, the item did not have a pre-existing Material Master Record. I created an ad-hoc PR using manual short text and assigned it to Account Assignment Category K (Cost Center 17101750).

  • The Block: When trying to convert it using the standard Process Purchase Requisitions Fiori app, the conversion button remained grayed out because a custom ad-hoc PR lacks a pre-assigned Source of Supply.

  • The Workaround: I bypassed the UI limitation by launching a clean draft in the Manage Purchase Orders app, entering the header data manually, and adopting the line items via the Add from Document function using the PR number. This generated PO 4500065403 flawlessly.

  • Here is Purchase Requisition
  •  
  • Here is the Purchase Order Information

  •  

2. The Goods Receipt Stage (MIGO)

  • Next, I opened the classic Post Goods Movement (MIGO) transaction engine to physically receive the fan.

  • By referencing PO 4500065403, the system successfully pulled the ad-hoc line item data. Marking the item as OK and posting the receipt instantly triggered a consumption posting to our cost center. This bypassed warehouse storage assets since the consumable fan goes straight into active use.

  •  

3. The Invoice Verification Stage (MIRO)

  • To close out the "3-Way Match," I logged the supplier's bill using the Create Supplier Invoice app.

  • The system referenced our PO data and verified that our ordered quantity, received warehouse quantity, and incoming invoice amount ($450.00 USD) balanced out perfectly. Posting this cleared the transaction smoothly into Accounts Payable.

  •  

Completing this entire lifecycle taught me so much about how flexible SAP S/4HANA is when handling non-standard business scenarios and how tightly logistics fields map directly to financial accounting modules.

Now that I have mastered the transactional P2P cycle, I am moving on to Master Data Management to learn how Business Partners (BP) are configured from scratch!

I would love to hear any tips or best practices from experienced consultants as I continue my journey!

Best Regards

entry.title: Explore SAP's New Joule Early Adopter Care Programs: Answers to Your Most Asked Questions
entry.content:
If you followed the announcements at Sapphire 2026, you've probably noticed that Joule is evolving rapidly. Between Joule Work, Joule Studio, and the new desktop experience, many customers are asking the same question:

"Where do I start, and which of these programs is relevant for my organization?"

To help answer that question, SAP has launched several Early Adopter Care (EAC) programs that give selected customers early access to upcoming Joule innovations while working directly with SAP product teams.

In this guide, I'll break down the four currently available Joule EAC programs, explain the differences between them, clarify common areas of confusion, and help you determine which opportunity best aligns with your business and AI adoption goals:

  1. Program Overview & What's New
  2. Registration & Process
  3. Eligibility & Prerequisites
  4. Joule Work & Joule Studio
  5. Joule with Knowledge Graph
  6. SAP Business AI Agents
  7. Joule Work Desktop App
  8. Trust, Data & Security
  9. Support & Next Steps
  10. Continue Your Joule Learning Journey

 

1 — Program Overview & What's New

Q1 I already have Joule — what's actually new, and why should I join an EAC?

Classic Joule (the version most customers have today) is a scenario-based assistant: you trigger predefined Joule skills within individual SAP applications — for example, asking Joule a question inside S/4HANA or SuccessFactors. It works well within an app context but requires users to know where to go and what Joule can do there.

The new generation announced at Sapphire 2026 is a fundamental shift in three areas:

  • Joule Work (Engagement Layer): A single, full screen entry point that sits above all your SAP applications. Instead of going to an app and then asking Joule, you express your intent directly and Joule surfaces the right data, agents, and actions from across connected systems. It introduces Spaces: dynamic workspaces that aggregate cross-application context for a specific goal. See how Joule takes action across systems to achieve people’s goals.
  • Agent Thinking: A new execution runtime replacing the legacy orchestration engine. It enables true agentic behavior: multi-step planning, tool calling, human-in-the-loop approval, and secure sandboxed execution — far beyond classic question-and-answer interactions.
  • Joule Studio 2.0: A managed development environment to build your own enterprise agents and skills, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and deployable to SAP's infrastructure. Deep dive into the new Joule Studio and watch the demo.

The EAC programs are your opportunity to use these capabilities before GA, work directly with SAP's product teams, and influence the final feature set.

Q2 Is this 'Joule 2.0'? How does it relate to the Joule I already have?

There is no product called 'Joule 2.0.' It is the same Joule — the core AI assistant technology has not been replaced or versioned. What has changed is how Joule is provisioned and experienced:

  • Provisioning change — SAP has moved from customer-managed Joule (where the customer set up Joule components in their own BTP tenant) to SAP-managed provisioning and Joule and SAP-managed integrations to products (SAP provisions and operates everything). This is a change to how you receive Joule, not a new product.
  • Joule Work — This is a new standalone interface that bundles Joule's existing AI capabilities with new features like Spaces and the front-end for Joule Studio, creating a single point of entry for SAP's key AI functionality. It complements, rather than replaces, classic Joule.

If you hear the term 'Joule 2.0' internally or from customers, it most likely refers to Joule Work — the new unified interface. Correcting this terminology matters because it avoids the impression that existing Joule investments are being deprecated or replaced with a completely separate product.

Q3 What Joule EAC programs are currently available after Sapphire 2026?

SAP launched four separate EAC programs. Each covers a distinct Joule product area:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio — The new AI-native engagement layer (Conversations + Spaces) and the AI-first development environment for building agents. Registration ends July 15, 2026. (Campaign #3986)
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph — Joule grounded in SAP's Business Knowledge Graph for precise, context-aware answers — plus upcoming agentic reasoning and voice capabilities. Registration ends October 1, 2026. (Campaign #4219)
  • SAP Business AI Agents — A broad portfolio of autonomous AI agents across HCM, Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, and CX. Continuously open registration. (Campaign #3987)
  • Joule Work Desktop App — A standalone desktop app for enterprise knowledge workers with local file access, web search, MCP integrations, and custom skills. Registration ends July 12, 2026. (Campaign #4218)

You may register for one or more programs. Registering is an expression of interest only and does not guarantee participation.

Q4 The Joule Work EAC and the Business AI Agents EAC both mention agents — what's the difference?

This is a common point of confusion. Here is how to distinguish them:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (#3986) — Focuses on the experience layer and the development platform. You use Joule Work as your unified workspace (Conversations + Spaces), and with Joule Studio you build custom agents and skills for your own organization. The agents here are ones you create or configure yourself.
  • SAP Business AI Agents EAC (#3987) — Focuses on SAP-built, domain-specific agents that come ready to use out of the box — pre-built assistants for specific business functions like a Payroll Assistant, a Sourcing Assistant, or a Deal Closing Assistant. Joule Assistants are role-based orchestrators that translate user intent into action by coordinating Joule Agents — which are the underlying, task-specific AI workers that autonomously execute complex, multi-step business workflows using tools, data, and reasoning.

An important nuance: the SAP Business AI Agents (Autonomous Assistants) only require Joule Work if they are system-triggered — meaning the agent initiates an action automatically based on a system event, without a user starting the conversation. User-initiated interactions with autonomous assistants do not require Joule Work. If you are evaluating autonomous agents, clarify whether your target use case is system-triggered or conversational, as this determines whether Joule Work is a prerequisite. All agents — whether system-triggered or conversational — require SAP-managed Joule, as it provides the Agent Gateway that powers agent execution. SAP-managed Joule can be set up independently of any EAC program via SAP for Me, and can either replace an existing classic Joule deployment or run side-by-side with one.

In short: #3986 is about building and experiencing the platform; #3987 is about consuming SAP's catalog of ready-made agents. Many customers will want both eventually.

Q5 Can we join multiple EAC programs at the same time?

Yes — the four EAC programs are independent and stackable. You can register for and participate in more than one simultaneously, provided you meet the individual prerequisites for each. The most common combinations are:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) + Business AI Agents (#3987) — for customers who want the new UX layer and SAP's pre-built domain agents.
  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) + Knowledge Graph (#4219) — for customers who want the new UX layer with deep grounding in their SAP data.
  • Desktop App (#4218) as an add-on — it has the lightest prerequisites and can run independently of the other three programs.

Note that each program has its own onboarding, project team requirements, and feedback commitments. Make sure you have the internal capacity to engage meaningfully with each program you join.

 

2 — Registration & Process

Q6 Where and how do I sign up?

All four programs are hosted on the SAP Influence portal at influence.sap.com. Log in with your SAP Universal ID, search for the campaign by name or number, and click 'Register.' Registration deadlines:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio — Register by July 15, 2026 (Campaign #3986)
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph — Register by October 1, 2026 (Campaign #4219)
  • SAP Business AI Agents — Continuously open (Campaign #3987)
  • Joule Work Desktop App — Register by July 12, 2026 (Campaign #4218)

If you experience issues with the portal, SAP recommends using the Chrome browser. For support, contact your SAP Account Executive or the EAC Program Lead.

Q7 I submitted my registration — what happens next, and how long until I get access?

Submitting the registration form is an expression of interest only. The process after registration:

  • SAP reviews your application against program criteria and available capacity. This typically takes a few weeks.
  • If accepted, SAP contacts you (via your Account Executive or the EAC Program Lead) to confirm participation and discuss onboarding.
  • Onboarding includes verifying prerequisites (e.g., SAP-managed Joule migration if needed), signing the EAC agreement, and scheduling a kickoff with your dedicated EAC coach.
  • If not selected in the first round, SAP may contact you as capacity opens.

For time-sensitive programs (Joule Work & Joule Studio closes July 15; Desktop App closes July 12), register as soon as possible to maximize your chances of being considered in the first intake.

Q8 When will Joule Work reach General Availability (GA)? Can we plan around a specific date?

SAP has not published a final, committed GA date for Joule Work or the other EAC programs. GA timing is intentionally contingent on the outcome of the EAC programs — SAP uses the co-innovation phase to validate readiness before committing to GA.

As directional guidance: EAC programs are expected to run for 3–6 months before GA. Based on the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC targeting a June 2026 start and running 180 days, a Joule Work GA in the Q4 2026 timeframe is the current directional target. For other programs with longer EAC windows, GA is expected in 2027.

For planning purposes, do not commit to hard internal deadlines based on these dates. Use the EAC period itself to build readiness, develop use cases in Joule Studio, and train your team — so you are ready to scale at GA regardless of the exact date.

Q9 How long do the EAC programs last?

Program durations differ per campaign. The Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC runs for 180 days from the program start (targeting a July 2026 launch, ending approximately mid-November 2026). The Knowledge Graph and Business AI Agents programs have longer or rolling windows. Exact end dates per customer depend on when participation is confirmed.

The EAC roadmap progressively unlocks additional Joule Work capabilities:

  • August 2026: Discover feed, Jobs supervision, System-generated Spaces (autonomous assistants), Joule Action Bar / Omnipresence
  • November 2026: Shared Spaces (multi-user + agent collaboration), Scheduled Jobs, Voice (web), Compositional Analytics

 

3 — Eligibility & Prerequisites

Q10 Is EAC participation free? What will Joule Work cost after General Availability?

EAC participation itself is free — there is no direct charge for the early access period. However, you must hold the relevant Joule entitlements and have signed the terms & conditions (available via SAP for Me). Depending on the track, a Joule Studio license and/or Joule Work Spaces activation may be required.

Pricing for Joule Work beyond the EAC has not yet been finalized and will be communicated separately ahead of GA. EAC participation does not commit you to any specific commercial terms for GA. Raise commercial questions with your SAP Account Executive early if they are a gating factor.

Q11 We are on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or on-premise — can we join the EAC, and do we need to migrate first?

S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (PCE) customers can participate in EAC programs — and importantly, you do not necessarily need to fully migrate away from customer-managed Joule before joining. SAP's recommended approach for existing customer-managed Joule customers is a hybrid brownfield setup: you register for the EAC and new Joule components (such as Joule Work) are set up in an SAP-managed way, while your existing customer-managed components remain connected. You do not have to do a full cutover before the EAC starts.

Program-by-program eligibility for Private Cloud customers:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition may become eligible with future product enhancements. At EAC launch, S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is the primary supported system. Register and SAP will assess your eligibility.
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph (#4219) — S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is explicitly listed as an eligible Joule setup. This is the most accessible EAC for Private Cloud customers.
  • SAP Business AI Agents (#3987) — Private Cloud and Public Cloud are both in scope. Note that specific agents and assistants require a certain S/4HANA Private Cloud version. A compatibility list of agents by S/4HANA Private Cloud version is available for PCE Finance and Procurement (May 2026). Contact your SAP account team or visit our agent roadmap for agent-specific version requirements.
  • Joule Work Desktop App (#4218) — No S/4HANA dependency at all, and equally no S/4HANA connectivity — you cannot read from or write to any SAP backend system. The app works with local files and external services via MCP tools only. The most broadly accessible program.

On-premise customers: On-premise S/4HANA customers can connect their system to Joule — and by extension to Joule Work — provided they meet specific criteria: a RISE contract and a commitment to the 40% maintenance fee. If these conditions are met, on-premise is a viable path into the EAC programs. The Knowledge Graph EAC (#4219) and Desktop App EAC (#4218) are the most relevant entry points.

Q12 We are a SuccessFactors-only customer — which EAC programs can we join?

SuccessFactors-only customers have access to specific EAC programs:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) — Not available at EAC launch. Joule Work Spaces currently requires SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. SuccessFactors integration into Spaces is planned but not in the initial EAC scope.
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph (#4219) — Open to SuccessFactors customers. Joule with SAP SuccessFactors is an explicit prerequisite option. Recommended EAC for SF-only customers.
  • SAP Business AI Agents (#3987) — Open. The Autonomous HCM domain is designed primarily for SuccessFactors customers — Core HR, Payroll, Recruiting, Performance & Goals, Learning, and more.
  • Joule Work Desktop App (#4218) — Open. No SuccessFactors or S/4HANA dependency, and equally no connectivity — you cannot read from or write to any SAP backend system..

Summary for SF-only customers: register for Knowledge Graph (#4219) and/or Business AI Agents (#3987) as your primary paths.

Q13 Is 'SAP-managed Joule' a prerequisite — and what exactly does it mean?

SAP-managed Joule is a prerequisite for the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC and for the Business AI Agents EAC. It is SAP's new SaaS delivery model where SAP provisions, hosts, and operates the entire Joule infrastructure on your behalf — replacing the older customer-managed model where customers maintained Joule components in their own BTP tenant.

If you are currently on customer-managed Joule, an upgrade is required before full Joule Work participation. However, SAP's recommendation is a hybrid brownfield approach: new Joule Work components are set up SAP-managed, while your existing customer-managed setup remains connected during the transition. You do not need to complete a full migration before registering for the EAC — raise your current setup with SAP during the acceptance process.

Q14 Can our system integrator or implementation partner participate in the EAC with us?

Yes — the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (Campaign #3986) is explicitly designed for customers and partners. System integrators and implementation partners can participate alongside the customer, which is the recommended approach if a partner is involved in your rollout.

  • Your partner can be involved in onboarding, configuration, and Joule Studio agent development.
  • The customer organization remains the primary accountable party — the dedicated project team and point of contact must be from the customer side.
  • Partners should coordinate with the customer and the SAP Account Executive during the acceptance process.

For the other three programs, partner involvement is not explicitly mentioned but is reasonable in an implementation capacity. Confirm with your SAP EAC Program Lead during onboarding.

 

4 — Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (Campaign #3986)

Q15 What is the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC and what can I experience?

This is the flagship EAC for the new Joule Work experience. It covers two products:

  • Joule Work — A full screen entry point framework connecting users to capabilities across SAP through intent, context, and conversational AI. The EAC includes Conversations (conversational AI with read and write access) and Spaces (intent-based, generative workspaces aggregating data across your SAP landscape).
  • Joule Studio — A fully managed, AI-first development environment for building enterprise AI agents, apps, and workflows. Supports intent-based development, open tooling (VS Code, Cursor, Vercel, n8n, LangGraph, LlamaIndex), and deploys to a secure SAP-managed runtime with enterprise governance, persistent agent memory, and access to the SAP Knowledge Graph.

You can participate in both together (Joule Studio license + Joule Work Spaces activation required) or in Joule Work only (Spaces activation required). Combined participation is recommended for teams who want to build and consume custom agents.

Q16 What are the prerequisites and known limitations for this EAC?

Prerequisites:

  • Signed terms & conditions for Joule and the $0 SKU for Joule Work / Joule Studio
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as primary LoB system
  • SAP-managed Joule (hybrid brownfield setup possible — see Q13)
  • Joule Work Spaces activation (required for all participants)
  • Joule Studio License (required for the combined track only)
  • A defined use case and dedicated internal project team with assigned point of contact

Known limitations at EAC launch:

  • Joule Work Spaces supports SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition only
  • Only one S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system per tenant (no multi-system)
  • Custom agent integrations limited to SAP-managed and SAP-native services — no third-party system integrations
  • Document grounding not supported
  • Spaces are initially read-only (write actions via Spaces in a subsequent update)
  • Datacenter Regions: USA and EU only

 

5 — Joule with Knowledge Graph EAC (Campaign #4219)

Q17 What is the Joule with Knowledge Graph EAC and who should apply?

This EAC gives customers access to Joule grounded in the SAP Business Knowledge Graph — enabling specific, context-aware answers based on your actual system data rather than generic AI responses, covering the full range of SAP entities. The program also gives early access to upcoming innovations including agentic reasoning and voice capabilities.

Prerequisites — an existing Joule setup with one of: SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors, or SAP Ariba. Your Joule instance must be in the EU10 or US10 data center (more coming soon). Registration closes October 1, 2026.

This program is the recommended entry point for Private Cloud and SuccessFactors-only customers.

 

6 — SAP Business AI Agents EAC (Campaign #3987)

Q18 What is the SAP Business AI Agents EAC and which agents are available?

This is a broad, continuously running EAC covering SAP's portfolio of autonomous AI agents across five business domains:

  • Autonomous HCM — Career & Talent Development, Compensation, Core HR, HR Knowledge, HR Service, Learning, Onboarding, Payroll, People Intelligence, Performance & Goals, Recruiting, Skills, Time, Workforce Upskilling.
  • Autonomous Finance — Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, Billing, Cash & Treasury, Financial Consolidation, Expense Management, Financial Closing, Financial Planning, Governance, Tax & Compliance.
  • Autonomous Spend — Buying, Category Management, Procurement Contracts, Invoicing, Sourcing, Supplier Management, Travel.
  • Autonomous Supply Chain — Asset & Service, Business Network, Logistics, Manufacturing, Planning, Product Design.
  • Autonomous CX — Campaign, Case Management, Deal Closing, Order Management, Sales, Service Management, and more.

Prerequisite: an existing Joule setup hosted in the EU10 data center (additional data centers coming). Note that specific agents require a certain S/4HANA Private Cloud version — consult your SAP account team for version-specific compatibility. Detailed prerequisites per individual assistant will be published by SAP in the near future. Registration is continuously open.

Important: the Autonomous Assistants only require Joule Work if they are system-triggered (agent initiates action based on a system event). User-initiated conversations with these agents do not require Joule Work.

 

7 — Joule Work Desktop App EAC (Campaign #4218)

Q19 What is the Joule Work Desktop App — and how is it different from the browser-based Joule Work?

Joule Work Desktop is a standalone native desktop application for enterprise knowledge workers. It is a distinct product from the browser-based Joule Work and is explicitly not integrated with the Joule Work web/cloud version.

Key capabilities: 

  • Conversational AI interface (on-device)
  • Local file & workspace access within a secure sandbox
  • Artifact generation — documents, summaries, structured outputs
  • Terminal command execution and integrated web search
  • MCP Server integration — connects to SAP and non-SAP systems via Model Context Protocol
  • Custom / corporate skills for repetitive tasks

Prerequisites:

  • SAP for Me access including an S-User ID
  • Requires download and installation of a binary executable on the user's machine — MacOS and Windows supported
  • A defined use case and dedicated project team
  • No existing Joule setup required — broadest accessibility of all four programs

Key limitations:

  • No integration with Joule Work (web/cloud)
  • No out-of-the-box SAP backend system integrations
  • Not available under EU-Access restrictions — must not be used for EU-only work purposes

Registration closes July 12, 2026.

 

8 — Trust, Data & Security

Q20 Where does our data go during the EAC — and is it used to train SAP's AI models?

Data governance is a top concern for enterprise customers. SAP's commitments in this area:

  • No training on customer data — SAP does not use customer business data to train or improve its AI models without explicit customer consent. This is a core SAP Business AI principle and applies to all EAC programs.
  • Data residency — For cloud-based EAC programs, data is processed and stored in your designated SAP data center (EU10 or US10). The data center region of SAP-managed Joule can be chosen during initial set-up
  • Joule Work Desktop — local data — The Desktop App accesses local files within a secure sandbox. Files are not automatically uploaded to SAP cloud systems. Review the specific EAC terms for what telemetry, if any, is collected.
  • Agent actions & HITL — All agent actions with business impact in Joule Work require explicit human approval (Human-in-the-Loop) before execution. No agent writes to your SAP systems without your confirmation.
  • Verifiability — Every action taken by Joule Work can be verified in the backend via traditional SAP Fiori apps, and AI recommendations include reasoning so users can validate before acting. All agent actions are written to the SAP Audit Log, providing a full traceability trail for compliance and governance purposes.

For contractual data protection terms, refer to the SAP Data Processing Agreement and EAC-specific terms signed during onboarding.

 

9 — Support & Next Steps

Q21 What support does SAP provide during the EAC — and what happens when the program ends?

All EAC participants receive dedicated co-innovation support:

  • Monitoring of critical issues by an EAC Project Coach and/or the development organization
  • Direct feedback channels — surveys, feedback sessions, and community forums — feeding directly into the Joule product teams
  • EAC-specific release notes and product update communications
  • Onboarding support to configure prerequisites and set up your first Spaces, skills, or agent scenarios

After the EAC: participants transition automatically to the GA versions. Your configurations, Spaces, and skills carry over. Pricing for Joule Work beyond the EAC has not yet been finalized and will be communicated separately. EAC participation does not commit you to any specific commercial terms for GA.

Continue Your Joule Learning Journey

As the Joule portfolio continues to evolve, investing in learning today will help your organization accelerate adoption and maximize value when these innovations become generally available. Here are several recommended resources to deepen your understanding and help your teams prepare for adoption:

  • Understand SAP's AI Strategy: Review the latest Sapphire 2026 announcements related to Joule, autonomous agents, and business AI innovation.
  • Learn about Joule: Introducing Joule Learning Journey focused on Joule capabilities and business user adoption.
  • Stay engaged through our AI enablement formats:
    • Beyond Hype, AI live session format, which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI
    • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills Monthly Blog Series spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

The latest Joule innovations are more than new features, they reflect SAP’s vision for an AI-powered, agent-driven enterprise.

Whether you're exploring Joule Work or Joule Studio, the Early Adopter Care programs offer a unique opportunity to gain early hands-on experience and collaborate directly with SAP product teams.

If you're considering participation, start with a clear business use case, review the prerequisites, and engage your SAP account team early. The most successful EAC participants are those who actively co-innovate and provide feedback that helps shape the future of these solutions.

All EAC programs are expressions of interest only and do not create binding commitments by SAP. Functionality, dates, and prerequisites are subject to change. For the latest information, visit influence.sap.com or contact your SAP Account Executive. This document reflects the program scope as of June 2026.

entry.title: Re: Fields are not visible in Enterprise Project Data Mig...
entry.content:
Dear @MananDurge,

I verified the case in my system by creating a new migration project EP in Migration Cockpit and next inside EP selecting the migration object Enterprise project

 

When opening the documentation directly from this migration object the In Scope section shows only:

  • Project General Data
  • Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)

I would therefore treat this as the currently applicable documentation for this migration object. This is also confirmed by the table structure within the migration project itself where only Project General Data and Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) are listed

If there is still any doubt, I would recommend raising an SAP Support case and attaching both SAP Help screenshots/links the downloaded XML/CSV template and the screenshot of the Table Structure from the migration object

More details below:

SAP KBA 1296527 - How to create a support case for SAP Support - SAP for Me

SAP KBA 1339209 - Best practices for creating a support case - SAP for Me

The second part concerns the missing additional fields in the template file, as I understand it.

In the Custom Fields app, I created the field below as an example

and in the UI section, I enabled all available views and Published

Inside the migration project, you will find the options Check for Updates and Check for Custom Fields for the migration object. Run both of them

Download the file and at the end of the template you should be able to see your custom field, for example mine shown below:

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Request Vendor & Customer Payment Receipt Advice Prin...
entry.content:
Hello!

Vendor Payment Advice (AP):

built into the Manage Automatic Payments app, using the Output Management framework (scope item 1LQ – Output Management). Vendor payment advice, processed through the Manage Automatic Payments app, uses output type PAYM_ADV. SAP delivers a standard email template called FFO_PAYM_ADVICE_EMAIL_TEMPLATE, which you can copy and customize to adapt the subject line and body.

Documentation below:

2941522 - Output Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Guided Answer

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/c5d60e507f584535b74ef5e11ea0c661.html?locale=en-US

https://support.sap.com/content/dam/SAAP/Sol_Pack/Library/Setup/1LQ_Set-Up_EN_XX.pdf

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/cdd1576869b644ae8a225f3849ed7a4e.html?locale=en-US

 

Customer Payment Receipt Advice (AR):

There is no standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud output type that generates and emails a "receipt confirmation" advice automatically the moment an incoming payment is posted against a customer. What is standard on the AR side is: creating correspondence to send invoices through various modes (PDF, email, etc.), and a Customer Statement correspondence type. That's an open-item statement, not a transaction-triggered "payment received" advice. Getting a true per-payment receipt notice to customers typically requires key-user extensibility (custom output type/BAdI built on the standard Output Management framework.

Documentation below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/411afe64471c4aaa99de461532b3d164.html?locale=en-US
